name: MHA
oneliner: The original transformer attention — each head independently computes Q, K, V over the full sequence, then results are concatenated.
explanation: Introduced in 'Attention Is All You Need' (Vaswani 2017). Input embeddings are projected into queries (Q), keys (K), values (V) separately per head. Each head operates on a lower-dimensional slice (head_dim = hidden_dim / n_heads), computes scaled dot-product attention independently, results are concatenated and projected. Different heads can specialize — syntactic, semantic, positional patterns. Used in virtually every transformer: GPT-2/3, BERT, T5, original LLaMA. The baseline against which all variants are compared.
fundamentals: For hidden_dim=4096, n_heads=32, head_dim=128: W_Q, W_K, W_V each [4096, 4096], W_O [4096, 4096]. Q·K^T / sqrt(128) → [batch, 32, seq_len, seq_len] attention matrix → softmax → multiply by V → [batch, 32, seq_len, 128] → concat → [batch, seq_len, 4096]. Param count: 4 × 4096² = ~67M per layer. KV cache per token per layer: 2 × 32 × 128 × 2 bytes = 16 KB. For 32 layers at 4096 tokens: ~2 GB. Compute: O(seq_len² × d_model) per layer — the quadratic cost.
sources: Vaswani et al., 'Attention Is All You Need,' NeurIPS 2017, arXiv:1706.03762

name: MQA
oneliner: All query heads share a single K and V head — reduces KV cache by n_heads× (e.g., 32×) with small quality tradeoff.
explanation: Introduced by Shazeer (2019) to address the KV cache bottleneck in autoregressive decoding. Each decoding step must read all cached K/V from memory — this bandwidth is the bottleneck, not compute. MQA uses one K head and one V head shared across all query heads. KV cache shrinks by a factor of n_heads (e.g., 32× for 32-head model). Quality degradation is small but measurable (0.1-0.5%). Used in PaLM, PaLM-2, Falcon-40B/180B, StarCoder. Led to GQA as a compromise.
fundamentals: For hidden_dim=4096, n_query_heads=32, n_kv_heads=1, head_dim=128: W_Q [4096, 4096], W_K [4096, 128] (32× smaller), W_V [4096, 128] (32× smaller), W_O [4096, 4096]. K and V broadcast to match query heads during attention. KV cache per token per layer: 2 × 1 × 128 × 2 = 512 bytes (vs 16 KB in MHA). For 32 layers at 4096 tokens: ~64 MB (vs ~2 GB). Bandwidth per decode step at seq_len=2048: ~32 MB (vs ~1 GB in MHA).
sources: Shazeer, 'Fast Transformer Decoding: One Write-Head is All You Need,' 2019, arXiv:1911.02150

foundational_papers.title: Fast Transformer Decoding: One Write-Head is All You Need

name: GQA
oneliner: Query heads grouped into G groups, each sharing one K/V head — the dominant middle-ground between MHA quality and MQA efficiency.
explanation: Introduced by Ainslie et al. (2023) at Google. Middle ground: multiple KV heads (but fewer than query heads), so each group of query heads shares one KV head. Can 'uptrain' MHA models to GQA for 5-10% of training compute and recover nearly all quality. GQA with n_kv_heads = n_query_heads → MHA. With n_kv_heads = 1 → MQA. The dominant variant in modern LLMs: Llama 2 70B (64Q/8KV), Llama 3, Mistral 7B (32Q/8KV), Mixtral, Gemma, CodeLlama, Yi. Typical ratio: 4:1 or 8:1.
fundamentals: For Mistral 7B: hidden_dim=4096, n_query_heads=32, n_kv_heads=8, head_dim=128, G=4. W_Q [4096, 4096], W_K [4096, 1024], W_V [4096, 1024]. Query heads 0-3 attend to KV head 0, heads 4-7 to KV head 1, etc. Implementation: K/V repeated via repeat_interleave or grouped computation. KV cache per token per layer: 2 × 8 × 128 × 2 = 4 KB (4× reduction vs MHA, 8× larger than MQA). General formula: cache = 2 × n_kv_heads × head_dim × seq_len × n_layers × bytes_per_param.
sources: Ainslie et al., 'GQA: Training Generalized Multi-Query Transformer Models,' EMNLP 2023, arXiv:2305.13245

foundational_papers.title: GQA: Training Generalized Multi-Query Transformer Models from Multi-Head Checkpoints

name: MLA
oneliner: DeepSeek's approach: compress K/V into a low-rank latent vector before caching — achieves MQA-level cache size with MHA-level quality.
explanation: Introduced in DeepSeek-V2 (2024). Instead of reducing KV heads (GQA/MQA), MLA compresses KV representations into a low-dimensional latent space via learned down-projection. Only the compressed latent is cached. At attention time, it's up-projected back to full K/V. DeepSeek-V2: 93.3% smaller KV cache than MHA while matching quality. RoPE is handled separately (position-dependent info can't be absorbed into low-rank compression). Used in DeepSeek-V2, V2-Lite, V3. Fundamentally different design philosophy: reduce dimensionality, not head count.
fundamentals: For DeepSeek-V2: d=5120, n_heads=128, head_dim=128, d_c=512 (compression dim), d_R=64 (RoPE dim). Compress: c_KV = x · W_DKV [5120→512]. Cache only c_KV (512 floats) + k_rope (64 floats) per token per layer = 576 × 2 = 1,152 bytes. Decompress at attention time: K = c_KV · W_UK [512→128×128], V = c_KV · W_UV. Final key = [K_content; k_rope] (dim 192). Compare: MHA=65,536 bytes, GQA(8kv)=4,096 bytes, MQA=512 bytes, MLA=1,152 bytes. Tradeoff: adds compute for up-projection but saves bandwidth — net win for memory-bound decoding.

foundational_papers.title: DeepSeek-V2: A Strong, Economical, and Efficient Mixture-of-Experts Language Model

name: SWA
oneliner: Each token attends only to a fixed-size local window of W preceding tokens — reduces quadratic cost to linear, with global reach through layer stacking.
explanation: Each token attends to at most W preceding tokens instead of all. After L layers, effective receptive field = L × W (information propagates through depth). Mistral 7B: W=4096, L=32 → 131K theoretical receptive field. Key benefit: KV cache is bounded at W tokens (rolling buffer) regardless of generated sequence length. Roots in Longformer (2020) and Sparse Transformers (2019). Modern architectures often use hybrids: Gemma 2 and later Mistral models alternate SWA layers with full-attention layers for direct long-range access plus cheap local patterns.
fundamentals: Token at position i attends to [max(0, i-W+1), ..., i] instead of [0, ..., i]. Attention mask: band matrix, not lower-triangular. Memory for attention scores: O(n×W) vs O(n²). Rolling KV cache: fixed buffer of W entries, oldest overwritten. For Mistral 7B (8 KV heads, head_dim=128, W=4096): cache per layer = 2 × 8 × 128 × 4096 × 2 = 16 MB; 32 layers = 512 MB constant regardless of generation length. Hybrid approach: even layers = SWA (W=4096), odd layers = full attention.

name: SDPA
oneliner: PyTorch's fused attention primitive — auto-dispatches to Flash Attention, Memory-Efficient, or math fallback based on hardware and input constraints.
explanation: torch.nn.functional.scaled_dot_product_attention (PyTorch 2.0+). Fuses Q·K^T scaling, masking, softmax, dropout, V multiplication into one kernel call. Before SDPA, users wrote attention manually or used xformers. Now the default in HuggingFace Transformers (v4.36+). Backend dispatch order: 1) Flash Attention (requires CUDA, fp16/bf16, head_dim≤256, no arbitrary mask). 2) Memory-Efficient (CUDA, arbitrary masks). 3) Math fallback (CPU or unsupported dtypes, materializes full N×N matrix).
fundamentals: API: F.scaled_dot_product_attention(query, key, value, attn_mask=None, is_causal=False, scale=None). Shapes: Q [batch, n_heads, seq_q, head_dim], K/V [batch, n_heads, seq_kv, head_dim]. Override backend: torch.backends.cuda.sdp_kernel(enable_flash=True, enable_mem_efficient=False, enable_math=False). Performance on A100 seq_len=2048: Math=baseline, Memory-Efficient=2-3× faster, Flash=3-5× faster.

name: Flash Attention (FA / FA2 / FA3)
expansion: Flash Attention — IO-aware tiling algorithm for exact attention
oneliner: Computes exact attention without materializing the N×N matrix in GPU HBM — uses tiling and online softmax to reduce memory from O(N²) to O(N), achieving 2-4× speedups.
explanation: By Tri Dao et al. (2022). The insight: standard attention is bottlenecked by memory bandwidth (reads/writes to HBM), not compute. The N×N attention matrix written to HBM is the culprit. Flash Attention tiles Q, K, V into blocks that fit in SRAM, iterates over K/V blocks per Q block, accumulates output using online softmax (streaming, numerically stable, no full-row knowledge needed). Result is mathematically exact — not approximate. FA1 (2022): tiling + IO analysis. FA2 (2023): 2× faster via better parallelism (over seq_len), better occupancy, 50-73% peak FLOPs on A100. FA3 (2024): targets H100 Hopper, exploits async WGMMA, TMA, FP8 support, up to 740 TFLOPS.
fundamentals: Standard IO: Θ(N·d + N²) HBM accesses. Flash Attention IO: Θ(N²·d²/M) where M=SRAM size. For typical M>>d², significant reduction. Tiling: for each Q block, iterate K/V blocks. Maintain running max m and sum l for online softmax. Rescale output O as new blocks processed. Memory: O(N) — only Q,K,V,O in HBM, no N×N matrix. Backward pass: recomputes attention from Q,K,V (gradient checkpointing). Versions: FA1 ~120 TFLOPS A100, FA2 ~230 TFLOPS A100, FA3 ~740 TFLOPS H100.

foundational_papers.title: FlashAttention: Fast and Memory-Efficient Exact Attention with IO-Awareness
FlashAttention-2: Faster Attention with Better Parallelism and Work Partitioning

name: KV Cache
oneliner: Stores computed K and V tensors from all previous tokens during autoregressive generation — avoids recomputation but is THE memory bottleneck for LLM serving.
explanation: Without caching, generating token t recomputes K/V for all t-1 tokens — O(n²) total compute. KV cache stores K/V from all previous steps; each new step only computes for the new token, appends to cache. Grows linearly with seq_len × layers × kv_heads. For large models at long contexts, KV cache dwarfs model weights. This is why MQA/GQA/MLA exist (shrink cache), why PagedAttention was invented (reduce waste), and why SWA bounds cache. KV cache quantization (to int8/int4) is increasingly common in production.
fundamentals: Formula: KV_cache_bytes = 2 × n_layers × n_kv_heads × head_dim × seq_len × batch_size × bytes_per_param. Examples (fp16): Llama 2 7B (32 layers, 32 KV heads): 16 KB/token/layer, 2 GB at 4K context. Llama 2 70B (80 layers, 8 KV heads): 4 KB/token/layer, 1.25 GB at 4K. At batch=32, seq=4096: Llama 70B = 40 GB cache (28% of weight memory). At batch=128, seq=8192: 320 GB — exceeds model weights. Each decode step reads entire cache from HBM — this bandwidth is the bottleneck.
sources: Pope et al., 'Efficiently Scaling Transformer Inference,' MLSys 2023, arXiv:2211.05102

name: PagedAttention
oneliner: KV cache memory management borrowing OS virtual memory paging — eliminates fragmentation, achieves <4% memory waste, enables 2-4× higher serving throughput.
explanation: By Kwon et al. (2023) in vLLM. Problem: naive KV cache pre-allocates max_seq_len per request → 60-80% memory wasted. PagedAttention divides KV cache into fixed-size blocks (pages), each holding a few tokens (e.g., 16). A page table maps logical positions to physical blocks. Blocks allocated on demand, freed on completion, need not be contiguous. Eliminates internal fragmentation (small blocks) and external fragmentation (no contiguous requirement). Result: <4% waste, fitting many more concurrent requests. Enables copy-on-write for shared prefixes (system prompts). NVIDIA adopted the concept in TensorRT-LLM.
fundamentals: Example: Llama 7B, 512 KB/token cache, max_seq_len=2048, 24 GB free. Naive: 1 GB/request, 24 concurrent. PagedAttention (block=16 tokens, 8 MB/block): 3,072 blocks total. Request using 512 tokens = 32 blocks = 256 MB → 96 concurrent (4× more). Page table: request_i logical_block_j → physical_block_k. Attention kernel gathers K/V via page table indirection. Copy-on-write: shared prefix blocks referenced by multiple requests; copied only when diverging. Waste: <4% (only last partially-filled block per request).
sources: Kwon et al., 'Efficient Memory Management for LLM Serving with PagedAttention,' SOSP 2023, arXiv:2309.06180

foundational_papers.title: Efficient Memory Management for Large Language Model Serving with PagedAttention
